narrative: As a white male applicant with an undergraduate GPA of 3.50 and an LSAT score of 36, your profile shows some solid strengths, including upper-middle family income and plans to attend full-time, which aligns with the vast majority of students in the dataset at about 93%. However, the admissions model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated a 72.7% chance that you would fail the bar exam—well above the threshold for approval, leading to the rejection of your law school application. This prediction hinges on a few key aspects of your academic record that stood out as the strongest influences. The most significant factor pushing toward a failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, which is notably lower than the typical student's 5.5 average and signaled weaker performance in later undergraduate years when compared to peers. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile of 3, also below the dataset average of about 5.7, reinforcing concerns about consistent early academic standing relative to others. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.50—higher than the average of 3.21—worked in your favor, pulling the prediction somewhat toward success and highlighting one area where you outperformed typical applicants. Overall, these decile rankings carried the most weight because they reflect your position among schoolmates, which the model sees as a strong indicator of bar exam readiness, unlike your stronger GPA which helped but couldn't offset the rest. Your LSAT score of 36 was close to the average of 36.7, so it played a neutral role without swaying the outcome much. In essence, compared to students who typically pass the bar, your mid-to-lower decile placements in Years 1 and 3 placed you in a riskier group, despite other positives like your GPA and background. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, perhaps through post-baccalaureate options or targeted GPA improvement, and reapply after demonstrating upward trends in academic performance. Many applicants in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these exact gaps, so reviewing your transcripts for patterns and seeking advising from pre-law counselors could make a real difference next time.
